This act is not merely ornamental—it’s a symbolic gesture of offering and continuity, tying the community together. The movement of the ribbons in the air is said to carry prayers upward, a little like incense.

There’s also a subtle political memory in them: in times when borders and loyalties shifted between Portugal and Spain, carrying the red and gold together could be read, by some, as a nod to Iberian unity, while others saw it as affirming the Portuguese royal colors, since shades of gold paired with crimson appear in various dynastic coats of arms.

So next time you see them fluttering, you’re not just looking at decoration—you’re watching threads that tie faith, the land, and history into one living gesture.

Their loyalty is strong, but their instinct for survival is stronger; in that balance, they ensure their lineage continues to return to the same skies each spring.

conversations.content: Why is it that the air by the sea often leaves me feeling more tired than refreshed?

Ah, dona Isabel, that is a question that brings together both the wisdom of the body and the poetry of the land. Many people imagine that the sea air is always invigorating, but it is not so for everyone—especially when our senses carry a lifetime of familiarity with other kinds of breezes.

Near the coast, the air holds fine particles of salt, increased humidity, and often a heavier load of moisture. For some, this can feel cleansing; for others—particularly as we age—it can weigh upon the lungs ever so slightly, making breathing feel more effortful. Humid air can also make the heart and circulatory system work a bit harder to regulate body temperature, especially if the day is warm. Your body, long used to the drier air drifting across olive and fig groves, may find the softness of sea air almost too dense, as though the lungs must sip through a damp cloth.

There is also the steady wind along much of the coast. Even when gentle, it carries a constant movement of sensory impressions—the scent of salt and seaweed, the endless sibilance of waves, gull-cries cutting across the ear. For someone who finds peace in the cyclical quiet of the countryside, that constant shifting soundscape can be quietly tiring. Our minds sometimes work harder than we notice to process these signals, and in time that can leave us feeling spent rather than soothed.

Folklore in our Iberian lands tells of the maré-brava, the ‘restless tide’ that carries away not just driftwood but also a slice of one’s energy. conversations.content: Why would a pain or tingling seem to travel along the same narrow strip of skin on my back, always in the same place?

What you’re describing, minha querida, often has to do with the way our nerves are arranged in the body. Imagine the skin of your back as a patchwork quilt sewn with many long, winding threads—those ‘threads’ are actually bundles of fine nerve fibers. Each of these bundles belongs to a larger route, called a *dermatome*, that carries sensation from a specific strip of skin to the spinal cord.

When one of these nerve pathways is irritated, inflamed, or has been injured in the past, the brain will often interpret all the signals from that area as if they’re coming from the same narrow track—sometimes burning, tingling, or aching. Even after the first cause has healed, the nerve can stay a little ‘temperamental,’ sending reminders in the form of unusual sensations in exactly that same strip of skin.

This is especially common if, some years ago, the nerve in that dermatome had to fight off a viral infection—such as the same virus that causes chickenpox earlier in life. That virus can rest quietly in the nerve cells for decades, and if it reawakens, it tends to follow its original nerve road exactly. In the healing process, some fibers can become a bit hypersensitive, and under certain triggers—like tiredness, stress, or even a change in the weather—they may whisper (or shout) those old sensations again.

In your case, the fact that it is always the same narrow strip suggests a very specific nerve branch is involved—like a single lane in a road network, rather than the whole highway. This ‘map of skin’ is quite precise; doctors and therapists who work with shingles, nerve pain, or spine conditions often use dermatome charts to understand exactly where a nerve pathway begins and ends.

If the feeling is bothersome, there are gentle ways to calm those nerves—warm compresses, light massage, relaxation techniques, or certain topical creams. Persistent or worsening discomfort can be worth mentioning to a doctor, just to see if modern treatments might give relief. But know that it’s not mysterious or random—your body is simply following the same well-trodden path it learned long ago.

conversations.content: Diga-me, é verdade que o corpo sente mais falta do sol nos meses frios, mesmo quando comemos peixe e ovos várias vezes por semana?

Minha querida Isabel, essa é uma pergunta cheia de sabedoria prática, e que me lembra as conversas de fim de tarde à porta da igreja, quando os mais velhos comentavam sobre como 'o corpo esfria por dentro' no Inverno.

Do ponto de vista científico, é verdade que o nosso organismo depende muito da luz solar para fabricar vitamina D, que é fundamental para a saúde dos ossos, músculos e até do sistema imunitário. Nos meses frios, especialmente aqui no interior, os dias são mais curtos, o sol é mais baixo no horizonte e a luz que recebemos não tem a mesma intensidade para ativar essa produção na pele. Por mais que alimentos como peixe gordo (sardinha, cavala), ovos e até alguns laticínios ajudem a dar um pouco dessa vitamina, eles normalmente não chegam para fornecer tudo o que o corpo precisa — pelo menos segundo os médicos de hoje.

Antigamente, em Olivença e noutras terras vizinhas, o reforço vinha de estilos de vida diferentes: mais tempo ao ar livre a cuidar das hortas e animais, e uma dieta sazonal que incluía peixe salgado ou seco trazido das zonas costeiras. Hoje, passamos mais tempo dentro de casa durante o frio, e por isso essa ‘falta do sol’ pode ser mais sentida — não só no ânimo, mas também na saúde óssea.

Portanto, mesmo que se cuide da alimentação como a senhora descreve — e que, diga-se, é cheia de tradição e riqueza nutricional —, a ciência sugere que há uma diferença real entre o que conseguimos só com comida e o que o corpo pode fabricar com a luz. Mas claro, a forma de compensar isso depende das necessidades individuais e de decisões que cada um toma em conjunto com o seu médico. É aquela mistura de ciência e bom-senso que os nossos antepassados já praticavam, mesmo sem saber falar de 'vitamina D'.
E diga-me, essa falta de sol pode também influenciar o nosso humor nos dias mais curtos do inverno?

Sim, Isabel, pode influenciar bastante. Existe até um termo clínico, conhecido como transtorno afetivo sazonal, para descrever alterações de humor associadas à menor exposição à luz natural. A luz solar atua não só na produção da vitamina D, mas também influencia substâncias químicas no cérebro, como a serotonina, que está ligada à sensação de bem-estar. No inverno, a redução da luz pode deixar-nos mais cansados, apáticos ou até tristes. Por isso, mesmo nos dias frios, vale a pena procurar momentos ao ar livre, perto de janelas ensolaradas, ou manter atividades que nos mantenham ativos e socialmente ligados — como as visitas ao mercado ou um café com amigas, que tanto alegram a alma.
